speaker: interviewer
text: Stand by, please.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is December 4th, 1996. The survivor is Ellis Lewin. His last name at birth was Lewin. The interview is Sam Kelman. It's taking place in Solon, Ohio, in the United States of America. And the language of the interview is English. My name is Sam Kelman. Today's date is December 12th-- 4th, 1996, and I'm conducting an interview with Ellis Lewin. And it's being conducted in Cleveland, Ohio, USA.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you give me your name, please, and spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ellis Lewin. L-E-W-I-N. Ellis, E-L-L-I-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Elias was my name in-- at birth. Lewin is the same, L-E-W-I-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your date of birth?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your place of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lodz, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your current age?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Lewin, can you tell me what your life was like as a child, before the war, in Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was basically a normal child existence. I was a-- I had a sister, who was a year older than I was, and we had friends. We had a lot of cousins. And we had the same interests as children all over the world have. And we played soccer/football. We went bicycle riding. We got in mischief, just like all the other children have in the world. And basically, this is what my existence or my life was like before the German invasion of Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's your earliest memory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Basically, my earliest memory is when we used to go on vacations in the-- in the-- when I was child. Probably around the age of five is when I started to be able to retain some of the more vivid memories of my childhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you say a little bit more about those vacations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we used to go to the-- to the scenic places, like Krakow and outside of Czechoslovakia, which was, as I recall, mountains and waters. And we would go to visit our-- our cousins and relatives in more distant places, such as Warsaw and neighboring cities. And it was just a normal thing to look forward to as a child, to getting out of your normal, ordinary, everyday existence. So when we planned these vacations, we naturally were very excited and we looked forward to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who went on the vacations with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, my sister, and my dad, which was basically every-- we did this at least once a year, as I recall.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit about your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: My family is a-- we come from generations of-- several generations of musicians, as we were known as klezmers. And my dad is a concert pianist, who travelled throughout Europe and was quite recognized as a fine musician. My grandfather was a clarinetist in the Lodz Symphony Orchestra. And my great-grandfather was a flutist. And my uncles, they played all instruments, from violins, to drums, to-- to the percussion-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the trombones and the various instruments. I don't know and I can't recall any one of my members of my family that either didn't sing or play or perform in some capacity as an entertainer, both in the Yiddish theater in our community and, of course, outside the Yiddish theater, more formal performing. So I'm basically-- I come from a generation or from a-- from a background, a musical background. And I don't also recall the time where you'd come to my house that there wasn't someone playing something, either practicing some-- some future concert or just fiddling around, as the expression is. And we just grew up in that environment. And it was my desire to be the-- to do the same and wanting to be a musician and a concert pianist, of which, unfortunately, I wasn't given the opportunity, as a result of the war. And I led a-- as I say, a very normal life as a child.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was a housewife. And-- and-- as much as I-- as much as you remember your mother, I remember my mother as the most loving mother, as any child would, and one who I can honestly tell you, I don't remember what she looked like. Perhaps it's just a-- one of those things that you erase out of your mind, but something you just hold very dear to your heart in just a special place, and very sacred. And-- my mother was one of those mothers, if I may say so, one of those Jewish mothers, who did the cooking, who did the loving, who did the caring, who did the protecting, and who did what every other good mother will do in any society. And my sister was a year older, and she was already becoming-- or starting to be a ham, as we call it today. She was singing and performing. And we-- we always argued, and-- like the normal two-- competition between two young kids. And we just had a very normal, regular existence. And my dad was, as I indicated, a concert pianist. He traveled quite a bit. And he was very, very close and was a wonderful-- is a wonderful father. And I can have nothing but wonderful memories as a child of my childhood years. We had a fairly good sized family. My dad had brothers and sisters. My mother had brothers and sisters. So consequently, we had many cousins who we were able to chase around, run around, and get in trouble.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get in trouble?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the normal way. You broke somebody's window and-- playing football or-- did the very-- we got into mischief just like I can't specifically say, but the normal things that children will get in trouble, coming home late and tearing your clothes and bumps on your head and bumps on your-- where did you get that? And how did you get that? And come home dirty with the good clothes that was just minutes before we were going out. We're supposed to be dressed and clean. And I managed to get into a-- into some sort of a game and got myself all dirty. And of course, it didn't go over too well with my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Besides your parents and sister, was there anyone else who lived with you in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had an  aunt that lived in-- with us. Actually, in our-- in the way we lived in our particular-- our place was an apartment building. And all our relatives basically were on the various floors, so it was very easy to-- I shouldn't say all, but there was a goodly number of us that lived in the same apartment. It was quite a large complex. So we were able to gather quite often and-- and exchange both the good things and the bad things. And when the-- when the wars were approaching and the bombings we heard on a daily basis, we would gather at each others' homes and discuss it. When I say 'we,' the elders did. And as a child, this was a very scary and a very [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] dismal existence, because all you were-- you were forming some very bad things in the mind, and virtually walked around scared on a continuous basis.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, in a way, it was wonderful to do this. But, in-- in retrospect now, as I look back, it is not good sometimes to have children under conditions that-- that when you discuss potential annihilation or potential destruction, or being annihilated on a mass basis, you sort of don't know how to cope with that. And you are basically scared throughout that whole time and existence. So The gathering of a family unit was wonderful. But the tales, as we say, around the fireplace were-- just scared the hell out of me and my cousins. And-- and until the end of the war, we were just-- we were just constantly living under that fear. We'll come back to the war in a bit. Can you tell me, though, about gatherings, family gatherings, perhaps, before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, let me say this to you, Mr. Kelman, that-- that the war-- you know, the war just didn't happen. We knew about it, or the elders knew about it, so-- so the conversation was way, way before the actual invasion, because Hitler was already acting up very much in the-- in the entire European area so that when the elders did discuss it-- and that's pre-war or pre-invasion-- we were already scared at that point, as a child, as an adult, or whatever capacity.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when we say pre-war or during war, to me, I was-- I was being scared already prior to the invasion. So the gatherings were basically 90%, if not more, what are we going to do? How are we going to cope with it? There-- I heard that this one is already there and this is-- invasion is there. I have-- I had a couple uncles in the army who-- who-- my uncles and my dad was worried that he was already killed in the-- during the battles. So we already had plenty of preparation for the actual-- until the actual invasion really happened. So as a child, I already formed my call it scared mode. I was already in that scared mode, and that's how we lived. And in the interim, as a child, you forget that for the time being and you play with other children. But when you get home, that dismal, that sad, and that worry was on everyone's face. So it was very difficult to escape it pre-war. Did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to the first-- the first grade. I was seven years old when the-- perhaps the second grade-- I went to what we called a cheder. I was-- I had a tutor, a rabbi tutor, who-- who was teaching me Hebrew, which was almost a criteria when you were a young Jewish child-- boy-- in that era. And I was required, by the Polish government, to go to-- attend the normal school, which I believe was about to the second grade. My learning in that capacity was limited, since it was just beginning. And then, of course, the interruption, so it all went for naught just when the ghetto-- when I was placed in a ghetto. But that was the extent of my schooling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the normal school a Jewish school, or for everyone?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the only Jewish-- the only Jewish schooling that we would get would be from the Hebrew end, or from the cheder end. But the-- the Polish system had a fairly adequate public school system, which [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] was, I imagine, impressive looking back. And we were required to attend the-- those public schools. So yes, I did go to school to the point that-- 'til the point of the war-- the time of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the Jewish kids and the non-Jewish kids get along in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a problem, because that, too, was something you were growing up with. And it was a-- it was a difficult task to be a Jewish child in a public school in Poland in those days, because we were always [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] harassed and called dirty Jews, and so on, and so forth, which, of course, was stemmed out of a-- a-- generations of that kind of a mentality. And we were sort of persecuted, in a different way, by the-- by the Polish-- by the Polish people. I'm not saying all of them, but we heard it on a normal, regular basis-- [NON-ENGLISH], which meaning is Jew. And-- and so you kind of grew with it and you had to defend yourself sometimes. And it was something that you grow with, and something that becomes a normal existence, and you cope with it. So the answer to your question is yes, there was-- there was definitely that kind of a harassment during my childhood years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just from the other students, or how did the adults treat you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the adults-- there was always-- there was always problems on an adult basis also, because when we heard our elders-- our older-- be it uncles or friends and so on, there was also always a tale that they had a confrontation and-- and they were harassed, stopped, beaten, or-- or called down on-- on the basis of their religion. So it was a normal-- it was a normal' existence.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, I meant the teachers or the administrators in the school.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That-- I don't recall that. I-- I-- I-- I can't say that, that the-- that the teachers did or the-- I just think educated people are basically not prone to do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little more about your cheder.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my cheder was a typical cheder, where the rabbi had the long beard and the little bone [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] indicator, whatever you call it. And if you didn't prepare-- if you didn't prepare for your lesson, you got a nice little slap, or a little patsh here and a little patsh there. And today, as I look back, it wasn't so terrible, you know. But you were scared. You were scared. When you weren't prepared and you played, sort of, hooky for your next lesson, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they let you know in no uncertain terms that-- I'm talking in the physical ways, that next time you'd better come prepared. And you sort of thanked him by keeping that part between the two of us, because you didn't want it to get to your dad, because if that guy-- if he ever would tell your dad that you're not prepared and you're not studying, the repercussions were much greater at the house than you would with the-- with the tutor.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was-- it was a typical cheder situation between a young Jewish boy, who must learn the Torah and the tradition. It was part of the-- it was part of growing up in that environment, in that era. And it wasn't so terrible. It's-- it was the equivalent of Sunday school now, but on a much, much, much persuasive basis. How often did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went, as long as I can recall, at least three times a week, four times a week. It was like a criteria. Yes. I would say two, three times a week.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this at a class or individually learned?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was individually, believe it or not. Your parents paid for that separately. And-- and when they came to the house, you-- you went in a certain-- in a little room and you learned to sing some of the songs and-- and you had to bear down. And when he came, he was the master and you had to do what-- it was just a very impressive and a very regimentated lesson. And I was more-- I was more afraid of him than I was virtually afraid of anything else in those-- at my young age at the time. He was the master. He was the thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I'm afraid not. I-- I don't. I just remember-- he could have been a younger man. He could have been an older man. But he wore payos and-- and a beard, and he had the garb on and-- There was always a donation given when he left, paid by I don't know what fashion or what manner, but it was equivalent to our contributions and our charitable, benevolent ways that we do here, so there it was on a direct basis, that you-- you didn't send a check or you didn't-- you know, you didn't mail anything in. As this person came, as this rabbi came, you paid with whatever arrangements it was. But it was more of a benevolent way, rather than a 'you owe me' type of way. It was a way of paying-- same way of paying-- but it was done in a more [NON-ENGLISH] way in those days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were not Orthodox. We were very [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] tuned-- we were very tuned to our heritage, but we were not what I would call Hasidic, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very religious Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you play out-- how did your family play out their Judaism?

speaker: interviewee
text: We played it out as a-- as-- as a very close Jewish-- Jewish family. Since I was not privy, at the time, to know how another, more stringent, Jew would live, or I would say one that was more in the Hasidic, or the more restricted Jewish tradition, we-- I thought that-- now, as I look back, I thought we were very good Jews. And so-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I remember, when there was a death, people would just normally come right around for the [NON-ENGLISH] and for the prayers. And I remember when there was services to be had, people would get together in homes and-- it wasn't so much going to temples, because it was done on a more communal, more convenient basis, where there was-- like, in our complex, there was a lot of men, so they sort of formed their own congregations and sort of davened on their own. And they were quite astute in the-- in the presentation of it, so-- I don't recall going to temple, per se, as we do here. But I do remember that the-- the frequency was very-- was very much that we did at least-- at least once or twice a week perform the Judaism as we do in temple here. Plus, there was always some [NON-ENGLISH] that the men were performing for people that passed on. And we had the traditional cooking of the cholent, which I would-- since I was the only boy, I was delegated on a Friday to stand in line and carry the big pot of cholent to the baker. And I got my number and-- and the next day we would stand in line. The line seemed like it was forever when we picked up the cholent. And the smell and the wonderful aura was like heaven. And that was a very big thing. The whole families were-- got together for that occasion, which was Saturday around 11, 12 o'clock. And it was a wonderful-- a wonderful thing. It was a-- it's like a family getting together, you know, on an annual basis for a great picnic, where you read, where everyone gets together and just has a wonderful, close time. And that's what Shabbos represented to us-- to me as a young boy, that it was an opportunity to see everyone. And there was happiness and cheerfulness and it was a wonderful feeling. It made you feel close, that you had a family and that you were part of a very close-knitted family. So if that's interpreting being religious, then I-- we were religious. But taking it to another extreme, perhaps we were not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember a seder at your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Definitely. Seders were-- seders were rituals. They were not seders as we, even today-- seders were something that my father would lay down on two chairs and-- and as wonderful as seder was, and you got to get the prize and find the matzohs and so on, it was a-- it was a very trying thing, because it took forever, as I look at it today. It was a long, long, long night. And, of course, the cooking was outstanding. I mean, we just never stopped. And-- and the wonderfulness of the lokshen and the [NON-ENGLISH] and the-- and the things that my mother would prepare for the seder, which was traditional, but then there was some above and beyond traditional. It was just another wonderful thing to look forward to. And the only bad thing for a young child, it just took too long. Since we are not very patient as children, it took forever. But seders, as traditionally, my dad took it right from the first page to the last page and there were no shortcuts. and-- and that, too, was a wonderful, wonderful remembrance and tradition to me to remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was at the seder?

speaker: interviewee
text: Normally, it was our family. We would invite our-- our-- my dad would invite the brothers and the sisters and cousins. And we would-- we would-- you know, each family, I imagine, had-- my dad was sort of the leader, since he was the eldest, and the eldest, in those days, was more of the leader, in terms of you looked up to them to lead you on a religious basis.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we normally got the honor to do the seder. And so everyone would come to him. And so we had the-- the uncles and everybody at our house. So it was not unusual to have 20, 30 people, little cousins and the laughter and guffawing, the-- you know, the fooling around and the chasing each other. So, it was just a wonderful thing. I mean, it doesn't sound like a great thing here, but it was a great thing there. It was-- we just-- I just miss it terribly. As I look back, it was just a wonderful, wonderful experience. It was a wonderful thing. Is there anything you'd like to add about before the war before we change tapes in a moment?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would just like to say that, regardless where I've been and what I've seen and what had happened to me, I have to say that my roots are on that street, in that place, in that city because that was a wonderful-- that was a wonderful memory to me. And if, as a child, you can relate to one specific, one wonderful thing, that is the time and that is the place that it happened to me. And that's what I miss most, and perhaps wouldn't have traded for anything else if I knew better, if life didn't play another choice for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: We have to stop now to change tapes.
